docs.title: DisSCube Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the DisSCube documentation. DisSCube is the geospatial data engine for the DisSModel ecosystem.

docs.title: Documentation Structure
docs.text:
• Architecture: Deep dive into how DisSCube works, its pipeline, and the SQLite catalog system.
  • Guides: Practical examples on how to use DisSCube with Brazil Data Cube tiles and custom grids.

docs.title: Quick Install
docs.text: pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Core Workflow
docs.text:
1. Register your grids and raw data sources in the catalog.
  2. Define a declarative SpatialDerivation.
  3. Execute the derivation to generate Zarr cube variables.
  4. Load the variables for analysis or modeling.

docs.title: Catalog & Persistence
docs.text: The Catalog is the "brain" of DisSCube. It records everything the system knows without storing the actual pixel data.

docs.title: SQLite Schema
docs.text:
We use SQLite for its simplicity, concurrency support (ACID), and indexing capabilities.

Table Purpose Key Columns grids Master and local grid definitions id, data (JSON) sources Raw data pointers (Rasters/Tiles) id, bbox, data derived Variables generated by the engine spec_hash, tile_id, grid_id relations Parent-child grid mappings source_grid_id, target_grid_id

docs.title: The Hash System (spec_hash)
docs.text:
To guarantee that a variable named dist_roads in 2026 is the same as in 2024, we calculate a SHA-256 hash of the SpatialDerivation object:

  1. Source ID: The origin of the data.
  2. Grid ID: The spatial definition.
  3. Variables/Operators: The exact math applied.

If any of these change, the hash changes, and the system treats it as a different data asset.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text: By using SQLite indexes on grid_id and tile_id, DisSCube can find a specific tile among thousands in milliseconds, a task that would require loading and parsing a massive JSON file in the previous architecture.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: DisSCube is designed as a Data Cube Engine specifically optimized for Land Use and Cover Change (LUCC) modeling. Unlike traditional GIS systems that handle files, DisSCube handles Variables aligned in space and time.

docs.title: 1. Separation of Space and Asset
docs.text: In traditional workflows, a "Grid" is often tied to a "File". In DisSCube, a GridSpec is a mathematical definition of space (CRS + Resolution + Extent). Multiple files (Tiles) can belong to the same Grid.

docs.title: 2. Immutability & Provenance
docs.text: Every operation in DisSCube is tracked via a spec_hash. If you change the parameters of a derivation, the hash changes, and a new variable is created. This ensures scientific reproducibility.

docs.title: 3. Tiled Execution
docs.text: To support continental-scale data (like Brazil at 10m), the engine never loads the full grid into memory. It partitions the execution using BDC (Brazil Data Cube) tiles or custom spatial partitions.

docs.title: High-Level Data Flow
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Raw Data: GeoTIFF/SHP] --> B{Normalizer}
    B --> C[GridAligner: Reproject/Resample]
    C --> D[Aggregator: FillCell Logic]
    D --> E[VariableWriter]
    E --> F[(SQLite Catalog)]
    E --> G[[Zarr Store]]

docs.title: Pipeline Stages
docs.text: The DisSCube pipeline is a sequence of idempotent stages. Each stage receives a PipelineContext and returns an updated one.

docs.title: 1. Normalizer
docs.text: Handles the entry point of the data. - Vector: Loads the shapefile/GPKG into a GeoDataFrame. - Raster: Prepares lazy-loading parameters and validates coordinates.

docs.title: 2. GridAligner
docs.text: The most critical spatial stage. It ensures the source data matches the GridSpec exactly. - Reprojection: Uses rioxarray and rasterio to transform the source CRS to the target Grid CRS. - Resampling: Applies the selected operator logic (Nearest, Bilinear, etc.) to match the grid resolution. - Cropping: Clips the data to the grid's Bounding Box (BBOX).

docs.title: 3. Aggregator
docs.text: Applies domain-specific logic (FillCell operators). - Proximity: Calculates Euclidean distance transforms for vector features. - Zonal Stats: Aggregates point or polygon data into grid cells.

docs.title: 4. VariableWriter
docs.text: Persists the final result. - Format: Saves as a Zarr dataset with consolidated metadata. - Hashing: Calculates the spec_hash and content_hash. - Registration: Updates the SQLite catalog with the new DerivedVariable entry.

docs.title: Master Grids & Tiling
docs.text: To process continental-scale data without massive memory requirements, DisSCube uses a "Virtual Tiling" approach.

docs.title: The Concept
docs.text:
Instead of creating thousands of individual small grids (which pollutes the catalog), we define a few Master Grids and use Tiles as execution filters.

graph TD
    MG[Master Grid: 10m Brazil] --> T1[Tile 001]
    MG --> T2[Tile 002]
    MG --> T3[Tile N...]

    T1 --> P1[Process Partition 1]
    T2 --> P2[Process Partition 2]

docs.title: How it works in Code
docs.text:
When you call cube.derive(derivation, tile_id="XYZ"):

  1. The system fetches the GridSpec for the Master Grid.
  2. It fetches the SpatialSource for the specific tile to get its BBOX.
  3. It creates a Temporary Grid that has the same resolution/CRS as the Master Grid but is restricted to the tile's BBOX.
  4. The pipeline processes only this small window.
  5. The output is saved in a tile-specific directory, preventing file collisions.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Memory Efficiency: Process Brazil at 10m in 2GB of RAM by iterating tiles.
  • Parallelism: Multiple workers can process different tiles of the same Master Grid simultaneously without race conditions (thanks to SQLite and unique paths).
  • Consistency: All tiles are guaranteed to align perfectly because they all derive from the same Master Grid mathematical definition.

docs.title: Brazil Data Cube Integration
docs.text: DisSCube provides first-class support for the Brazil Data Cube (BDC) hierarchical grid system.

docs.title: Master Grids vs. Tiles
docs.text: The BDC partitions Brazil into tiles. In DisSCube, we represent this using Master Grids (the resolution definition) and Spatial Sources (the individual tiles).

docs.title: Available Master Grids
docs.text:
• BDC_SM: 10m resolution (Small tiles).
  • BDC_MD: 30m resolution (Medium tiles).
  • BDC_LG: 60m resolution (Large tiles).
  • BDC_100m: 100m custom resolution (Continental scale).

docs.title: Tiled Derivation
docs.text:
When you run a derivation, you specify the tile_id. DisSCube performs the following steps:

  1. Lookup: Finds the BBOX of the tile in the catalog.
  2. Crop: Restricts the Master Grid to that specific BBOX.
  3. Process: Executes the operators only for that area.
  4. Partition: Saves the Zarr file in a directory named after the tile.

This approach allows you to process a single tile for testing or iterate through all tiles of Brazil using a simple loop, without ever hitting memory limits.

# Processing multiple tiles in a loop
for tile in ["008013", "008014", "008015"]:
    cube.derive(my_derivation, tile_id=tile)

docs.title: Working with Custom Grids
docs.text: While DisSCube is pre-configured for BDC, you can define any grid system for your project.

docs.title: Creating a Local Grid
docs.text:
A local grid is usually a small area of interest (AOI) with a specific resolution.

from disscube.models import GridSpec

my_grid = GridSpec(
    id="MyProject/30m",
    type="local",
    crs="EPSG:31983",
    resolution=30.0,
    bbox=[580000, 9700000, 600000, 9720000],
    description="Custom grid for local study area"
)

cube.register_grid(my_grid)

docs.title: Creating a Master Grid
docs.text: A master grid is a continental definition. It doesn't need tiles to be registered, but if you want to use the tile_id feature, you must register the tiles as SpatialSource entities in the catalog.

docs.title: Grid Relations
docs.text:
You can relate grids to create hierarchies (e.g., 5km to 25km).

from disscube.models import SpatialRelation

rel = SpatialRelation(
    source_grid_id="br_5km",
    target_grid_id="br_25km",
    strategy="simple"
)
cube.register_relation(rel)

This allows the Aggregator to automatically know how to upsample or downsample variables between these two grids.
